_meta.authoringNotes: Congenital craniofacial condition. Causes/risk factors framed around prenatal exposures and genetics; treatment around staged surgical repair across childhood. Numbers checked against Mossey Lancet 2009 (PMID 19747722), CDC NBDPS data, ICBDSR global registries (Salari 2022, PMID 34033944), Smile Train and ACPA team care standards.

h1Title: Cleft Lip and Palate: Causes, Diagnosis, and Surgical Repair
heroOverview: Cleft lip and palate is the most common craniofacial birth defect, affecting approximately 1 in every 700 to 800 live births worldwide — roughly 220,000 newborns each year. It results from incomplete fusion of the embryonic facial processes between the 4th and 12th weeks of gestation, producing a gap in the upper lip, the roof of the mouth, or both. Severity ranges from a small notch in the lip border to a complete bilateral cleft extending through the alveolar ridge and the hard and soft palate. With coordinated cleft team care — combining plastic and maxillofacial surgery, orthodontics, speech-language pathology, audiology, dentistry, and psychology — the vast majority of affected children achieve near-normal facial appearance, intelligible speech, and full participation in school and adult life. Surgical repair of the lip at 3-6 months and of the palate by 12 months is the foundation of treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 700-800 live births worldwide; ~7,000 US infants born annually with orofacial clefts (CDC)
keyStats.demographics: Cleft lip with or without palate is 2x more common in males; isolated cleft palate is more common in females
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital — present at birth; surgical repair begins at 3-6 months for the lip and by 12 months for the palate
keyStats.globalCases: Asia 1.57 per 1,000 live births; Europe 1.55; Africa 0.57 — significant regional variation (Salari et al, 2022)

definition: Cleft lip and palate (ICD-10: Q35-Q37) is a congenital malformation arising from failure of fusion between the medial nasal prominence, lateral nasal prominence, and maxillary prominence during weeks 4 to 7 of embryonic development (for the lip), and failure of fusion of the palatal shelves during weeks 8 to 12 (for the palate). The condition is classified by laterality (unilateral or bilateral), completeness (incomplete or complete), and structures involved (lip only, lip and alveolus, lip and palate, palate only). Clefts are further classified as syndromic (associated with a recognised genetic syndrome such as Van der Woude, 22q11.2 deletion, or Pierre Robin sequence) or non-syndromic (isolated). Approximately 70% of cleft lip with or without palate cases and 50% of isolated cleft palate cases are non-syndromic; the rest are part of more than 400 described syndromes. The condition is managed by multidisciplinary cleft teams typically led by plastic or maxillofacial surgeons, with coordinated input from orthodontists, otolaryngologists, speech-language pathologists, audiologists, pediatric dentists, geneticists, social workers, and psychologists. Care extends from prenatal counselling through adolescence and into adulthood when secondary procedures (rhinoplasty, orthognathic surgery) are needed.

typesClassification.description: A gap on one side of the upper lip. Incomplete forms a notch; complete extends to the nostril floor. Most common single-cleft presentation; left-sided is roughly twice as common as right-sided.
Gaps on both sides of the upper lip with a protruding central premaxilla. Surgical repair is staged and presents greater technical challenge than unilateral; modern outcomes are excellent.

Gap in the roof of the mouth, ranging from soft palate alone to complete soft and hard palate. More common in females and in syndromic forms; often associated with Pierre Robin sequence and 22q11.2 deletion.

typesClassification.type: Cleft lip and palate (unilateral or bilateral)
typesClassification.description: Combined cleft extending from lip through alveolus to hard and soft palate. The most extensive form; requires staged surgical repair beginning in infancy and continuing through adolescence.
Apparent palate intact but with a defect in the muscular layer (bifid uvula, palatal notch, midline translucency). Often diagnosed later when speech or feeding issues emerge.

primarySymptoms: Visible split or gap in the upper lip ranging from a small notch in the vermilion border to a wide complete cleft extending into the nostril floor — apparent at birth or on prenatal ultrasound.

Visible opening in the roof of the mouth (palate) — examined by gently inspecting the infant's mouth at the newborn assessment; may extend from the soft palate forward through the hard palate and alveolus.

Feeding difficulties — inability to generate adequate intraoral suction means infants cannot breastfeed or use standard bottles effectively; special bottles with one-way valves (Haberman, Pigeon, Dr Brown's specialty feeders) are needed.
Nasal regurgitation — milk coming out of the nose during feeding due to lack of seal between the oral and nasal cavities.
Nasal-sounding (hypernasal) speech once the child begins talking, particularly if palate repair has not occurred or has not produced complete velopharyngeal closure.

Recurrent middle ear infections (otitis media) and hearing loss — eustachian tube dysfunction is near-universal in cleft palate; over 90% of infants need ear tubes (myringotomy with grommets) by age 2.
Dental anomalies including missing, extra, or malformed teeth at the cleft site, malocclusion, and crowding requiring orthodontic intervention.
Slow weight gain in early infancy if feeding strategy is not adapted promptly — cleft teams provide feeding consultation in the first days of life.
Cosmetic concerns about lip shape, nostril asymmetry, and scarring after lip repair — many children undergo secondary lip and nasal revisions in adolescence or early adulthood.
earlyWarningSigns: Detection of a facial cleft on prenatal ultrasound at the 18-22 week anomaly scan — allows planning and family preparation before delivery
Bifid uvula or palpable notch in the hard palate on newborn examination — may indicate submucous cleft requiring later assessment
Difficulty with breastfeeding latch or audible air loss during feeding in the newborn period
Persistent middle ear effusion or recurrent otitis media in a child without obvious anatomical cleft — prompts assessment for submucous cleft
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory distress, cyanosis, or apnea in a newborn with cleft palate — possible airway obstruction from Pierre Robin sequence; requires immediate neonatal assessment
Severe feeding difficulty with failure to gain weight in the first 2 weeks of life — needs urgent cleft team feeding support and may require nasogastric supplementation
Postoperative bleeding, fever, or wound dehiscence after lip or palate repair — same-day surgical review
Sudden hearing loss or severe ear pain in a child with a cleft — possible acute otitis media or grommet complication requiring ENT assessment

causes.description: Most non-syndromic clefts result from the combined effect of multiple low-effect genetic variants plus environmental triggers. Twin studies show 40-60% heritability for cleft lip with or without palate; concordance is roughly 40% in monozygotic twins and 5% in dizygotic twins.

Approximately 30% of cleft lip and 50% of isolated cleft palate cases are part of a recognised syndrome. Major examples include Van der Woude (IRF6 mutation, autosomal dominant), 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge/velocardiofacial), Stickler syndrome, and Treacher Collins.

Smoking during the first trimester increases the risk of orofacial cleft by roughly 30-50%. Effect is dose-dependent and is one of the best-established preventable environmental causes; quitting before pregnancy fully eliminates the risk.

Heavy and binge drinking in early pregnancy raises cleft risk, often as part of broader fetal alcohol spectrum effects. Mechanism involves disruption of neural crest cell migration and craniofacial morphogenesis.

Anti-epileptic drugs (phenytoin, valproate, topiramate), methotrexate, and high-dose vitamin A derivatives (isotretinoin) are established teratogens. Valproate raises cleft palate risk roughly 5-fold; preconception medication review is critical.

Inadequate periconceptional folate intake is linked to higher cleft risk in observational data, with about 20-30% lower risk in women taking 400 µg folic acid daily before conception. Universal supplementation is recommended.

Pregestational type 1 and type 2 diabetes raises cleft risk roughly 2-3 fold, likely through hyperglycemia-induced disruption of craniofacial development. Optimal glycemic control before conception lowers the risk.
BMI ≥30 before pregnancy is associated with modestly higher cleft risk (1.2-1.4 fold), partly mediated by metabolic factors and folate metabolism.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of cleft in a first-degree relative
riskFactors.description: Risk in a sibling rises to roughly 3-5% (versus 0.1% population baseline); risk in offspring of an affected parent is 2-4%. Genetic counselling is offered to all affected families.
Cleft lip with or without palate is roughly twice as common in males; isolated cleft palate is more common in females.
Highest population prevalence in American Indian (2.6 per 1,000 live births) and Asian populations (1.5-1.8 per 1,000); lowest in African populations (0.4-0.6 per 1,000).
Increases risk by 30-50%; effect dose-dependent. Quitting before conception eliminates this excess risk.
Heavy and binge drinking raises cleft risk; safest course is complete avoidance from conception.

riskFactors.factor: Maternal anti-epileptic and other teratogenic medications
riskFactors.description: Valproate, topiramate, phenytoin, methotrexate, and isotretinoin raise cleft risk; preconception medication review and switch to safer alternatives where possible.
Roughly 2-3 fold higher cleft risk; tight glycemic control before conception substantially reduces this.
Associated with 20-40% higher cleft risk; weight management before conception is beneficial.
Periconceptional 400 µg folic acid daily reduces cleft risk by 20-30% in observational studies.
Modest increase in cleft risk with advanced maternal age, especially when combined with chromosomal anomalies.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 7,000 infants born in the US each year with orofacial clefts (CDC NBDPS)
Global prevalence approximately 1 in 700 live births, with the highest rates in American Indian, Asian, and Latin American populations
Cleft lip with or without palate is roughly twice as common in males; isolated cleft palate is more common in females
Left-sided unilateral clefts outnumber right-sided clefts approximately 2:1
More than 85% of children born with clefts live in low- and middle-income countries, where access to surgical care is more limited than in high-income settings
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cleft lip is most often made on prenatal ultrasound, typically at the routine 18-22 week anomaly scan, where the facial profile and upper lip are evaluated in coronal and axial views. Detection rates vary widely depending on equipment and operator experience: cleft lip is identified prenatally in 30-65% of cases in most populations, while isolated cleft palate is detected in under 10% because the bony palate is poorly visualized by 2D ultrasound. 3D and 4D ultrasound improve visualization. When a cleft is suspected prenatally, referral to a fetal medicine specialist confirms the diagnosis, screens for additional anomalies, and arranges genetic counselling and amniocentesis if a syndromic cause is suspected. Postnatally, the diagnosis is established on visual inspection of the lip and direct examination of the palate during the newborn assessment — a gloved finger sweep of the hard and soft palate detects submucous clefts that may not be visible. Within the first days of life, the infant is assessed by the cleft team for feeding adequacy, weight gain, airway (especially in Pierre Robin sequence), and other associated anomalies. Cardiac evaluation, renal ultrasound, and skeletal survey are considered when a syndrome is suspected. Genetic testing — typically chromosomal microarray and targeted gene panels (IRF6, TBX22, MSX1) — is offered when there are dysmorphic features, family history, or additional anomalies. Hearing assessment with auditory brainstem response is performed by 1 month because conductive hearing loss is near-universal with cleft palate. Speech-language assessment begins at age 12-18 months once palate repair is complete and continues at intervals throughout childhood.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects cleft lip in 30-65% of cases on routine 2D scan; 3D/4D ultrasound and dedicated fetal medicine views improve accuracy. Identifies additional anomalies that may suggest a syndromic cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard antenatal scan with the mother lying back, transabdominal probe, and approximately 30-45 minute appointment. Sometimes followed by a higher-resolution scan in fetal medicine if a cleft is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visual inspection of the lip and finger-palpation of the hard and soft palate to identify cleft lip, cleft palate, or submucous cleft. Part of every routine newborn check.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless examination performed in the first 24-72 hours of life. Takes a few minutes; gloves and a light source are used. Findings are explained to parents and the cleft team is notified immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Cleft team feeding and airway assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines optimal bottle and feeding strategy, evaluates for airway compromise (especially Pierre Robin sequence), and monitors weight gain in the first weeks of life.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Multidisciplinary assessment in the first week of life — typically a feeding specialist, nurse, surgeon, and sometimes an ENT specialist. Parents are taught specialty bottle use; weights are checked frequently.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic testing (chromosomal microarray, targeted gene panels)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies syndromic causes (22q11.2 deletion, Van der Woude IRF6 mutation, Stickler) in children with additional anomalies, family history, or dysmorphic features. Guides recurrence risk counselling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or buccal swab sample; results typically return in 2-6 weeks. Genetic counselling appointments are part of standard care for affected families.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects sensorineural and conductive hearing loss early. Eustachian tube dysfunction is near-universal in cleft palate, so hearing is monitored from birth.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed while the infant sleeps, typically in an outpatient audiology clinic. Soft sounds are played and electrodes record the auditory response. Painless, takes 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses velopharyngeal function in older children with hypernasal speech after palate repair, to plan secondary procedures (pharyngeal flap, sphincter pharyngoplasty).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible scope is passed through the nose; the child says specific speech sounds while the clinician watches the palate move. Performed in clinic; mildly uncomfortable for 1-2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Dental and orthodontic radiographs (panoramic and cephalometric X-ray)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Plans orthodontic and orthognathic surgery during the mixed dentition (age 7-10) and adolescent stages. Identifies missing or supernumerary teeth and quantifies skeletal jaw discrepancies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard outpatient dental imaging; painless and takes a few minutes. Low radiation dose.

treatmentOverview: Cleft lip and palate is treated through a coordinated sequence of surgical and non-surgical interventions across childhood and adolescence, delivered by a multidisciplinary cleft team. The standard timeline in high-income settings is: feeding support and nasoalveolar molding (NAM) in the first weeks of life; cleft lip repair (cheiloplasty) at 3-6 months; cleft palate repair (palatoplasty) by 12-18 months; ventilation tubes (grommets) for middle ear effusion in the first 2 years and as needed; speech therapy from age 2; orthodontic intervention during mixed dentition (age 7-10); alveolar bone grafting at age 7-11 to provide bone for canine eruption; secondary speech procedures (pharyngeal flap, sphincter pharyngoplasty) if velopharyngeal insufficiency persists; definitive orthodontics and orthognathic surgery (Le Fort I osteotomy) for jaw discrepancies during late adolescence; and rhinoplasty or lip revision at skeletal maturity (age 16-18). The Millard rotation-advancement technique remains the most common unilateral cleft lip repair; Mulliken technique is widely used for bilateral repair. Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty and intravelar veloplasty are the dominant palatoplasty techniques. Modern repair achieves intelligible speech in over 80% of children, normal facial appearance in over 90%, and near-normal social and academic functioning in the great majority. Smile Train, Operation Smile, and ACPA-certified cleft teams provide the gold-standard pathway in resource-limited settings. Treatment success depends as much on long-term multidisciplinary coordination, family engagement, and access to speech therapy as on the technical surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Specialty bottle feeding (Haberman, Pigeon, Dr Brown's cleft feeder)
medicalTreatments.description: Pre-vented bottles with one-way valves that deliver milk without requiring intraoral suction. Started in the first 24-48 hours of life; cleft team feeding specialists train parents.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate weight gain achieved in over 95% of infants when used correctly; reduces nasogastric tube need to under 5%.

medicalTreatments.name: Nasoalveolar molding (NAM)
medicalTreatments.description: Custom-fitted oral appliance worn for 3-4 months before lip repair to bring cleft segments closer together, reshape the nasal cartilage, and elongate the columella. Used selectively in wide unilateral and bilateral clefts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cleft gap by 30-50% before lip repair; improves nasal symmetry and reduces need for secondary nasal surgery in observational cohorts.

medicalTreatments.name: Ventilation tubes (myringotomy with grommets)
medicalTreatments.description: Small tubes placed across the eardrum to drain middle ear fluid and ventilate the middle ear in children with chronic effusion. Performed at the time of palate repair or separately when needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves middle ear effusion in over 90% of children with cleft palate; reduces conductive hearing loss and supports speech development.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech-language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Started by age 12-18 months after palate repair and continued through childhood. Targets articulation, resonance, and compensatory misarticulations. Intensive blocks of therapy alternate with maintenance sessions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Intelligible speech achieved in over 80% of children with isolated cleft palate by age 6 when delivered by cleft-team-trained speech pathologists.

medicalTreatments.name: Orthodontic treatment
medicalTreatments.description: Begins in mixed dentition (age 7-10) with maxillary expansion before alveolar bone grafting, continues through adolescence with comprehensive fixed appliances. Cleft team orthodontists coordinate with surgeons throughout.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Functional occlusion achieved in over 90% of cases by adulthood; cosmetic and functional results comparable to non-cleft orthodontic patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Genetic counselling
medicalTreatments.description: Offered to all affected families. Provides recurrence risk estimates, screens for syndromic causes, and supports informed reproductive decisions. Recurrence risk in siblings is 3-5% for non-syndromic clefts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves family understanding and reproductive planning in over 90% of consulted families; identifies syndromic diagnoses missed on clinical exam in roughly 10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Cleft lip repair (cheiloplasty) — Millard rotation-advancement or Mohler modification
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical closure of the cleft lip and nasal floor performed at 3-6 months of age. The Millard rotation-advancement technique remains most common; Mulliken or other techniques are used for bilateral clefts. Operating time 1.5-3 hours under general anesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Primary lip repair achieves good or excellent cosmetic results in over 90% of cases; secondary lip revision rate approximately 10-30% during childhood or adolescence.

surgicalOptions.name: Cleft palate repair (palatoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical closure of the hard and soft palate at 9-18 months of age. Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty and intravelar veloplasty (IVV) are the dominant techniques; technique selection depends on cleft type and surgeon preference. Operating time 1.5-3 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Velopharyngeal competence with intelligible speech achieved in 75-90% of cases at first repair; 10-25% need secondary speech surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Alveolar bone grafting
surgicalOptions.description: Transfer of cancellous bone (typically from the iliac crest) into the alveolar cleft at age 7-11, before canine eruption. Provides bone support for the canine to erupt naturally and stabilizes the dental arch.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful bony union achieved in 85-95% of grafts when performed in the optimal age window; canine erupts into the graft in over 85% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Secondary speech surgery for persistent velopharyngeal insufficiency after palate repair. Creates additional anatomic closure between the palate and posterior pharyngeal wall to eliminate hypernasal speech.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of hypernasality in 75-90% of cases; secondary procedure risk includes airway obstruction during sleep.

surgicalOptions.name: Le Fort I orthognathic surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Maxillary advancement performed in late adolescence (age 16-18) to correct mid-face hypoplasia, which affects 25-50% of patients with complete clefts. Coordinated with orthodontics and often combined with mandibular surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional occlusion and improved facial aesthetics in over 90% of cases; some patients need distraction osteogenesis for larger advancements.

surgicalOptions.name: Secondary lip and nose revision (rhinoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Refinement procedures for residual lip asymmetry, nostril asymmetry, or septal deviation. Performed at skeletal maturity (age 16-18) or earlier for major functional or social concerns.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient and family satisfaction in over 85% of cases; many patients have multiple staged revisions over years.

alternativeTreatments.name: Periconceptional folic acid supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: 400-1,000 µg daily folic acid starting at least 1 month before conception lowers cleft risk by 20-30% in observational studies and is universally recommended.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking and alcohol cessation before pregnancy
alternativeTreatments.description: Eliminating tobacco and alcohol exposure before conception removes two of the strongest preventable risk factors for orofacial clefting.

whySeeSpecialist: Every child born with a cleft should be cared for by a multidisciplinary cleft team — ACPA-certified in the US or equivalent national accreditation elsewhere. The team includes plastic or maxillofacial surgeons, orthodontists, otolaryngologists, speech-language pathologists, audiologists, pediatric dentists, geneticists, nurses, and psychologists. Team-based care delivers better speech, dental, and psychosocial outcomes than fragmented care. Prenatal diagnosis triggers referral to a fetal medicine specialist; postnatal diagnosis triggers cleft team referral within the first week of life.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an ACPA-approved cleft team (US) or equivalent national accreditation. Look for surgeons with high annual volumes (at least 30-50 cleft procedures per year) and a published outcomes record. Continuity of care matters — children typically need 10-15 years of follow-up; team stability and coordination predict outcomes more than any single surgeon's reputation. In low- and middle-income countries, Smile Train and Operation Smile partner sites provide free care meeting international standards.
preventionStrategies: Take 400-1,000 µg folic acid daily starting at least 1 month before conception — reduces cleft risk by 20-30%
Stop smoking before conception — eliminates the 30-50% excess cleft risk associated with first-trimester smoking
Avoid alcohol from the time pregnancy is planned
Review all prescription medications with a clinician before pregnancy; switch teratogenic agents (valproate, topiramate, methotrexate, isotretinoin) to safer alternatives where possible
Optimize diabetes control (HbA1c <6.5%) before conception in women with pre-existing diabetes
Achieve healthy weight before pregnancy where possible — maternal obesity raises cleft risk by 20-40%
Attend genetic counselling if there is a family history of cleft or known cleft-associated syndrome
lifestyleModifications: Engage early with the cleft team and follow the recommended surgical and therapy timeline closely — better adherence predicts better long-term outcomes
Use specialty cleft feeding bottles as taught; expect and prepare for longer feeding times (30-45 minutes per feed) in the early weeks
Attend every speech-language therapy session and complete home practice — speech outcomes depend heavily on therapy adherence
Maintain rigorous oral hygiene with cleft-team pediatric dental input — children with clefts have higher caries risk at the cleft site
Watch for ear symptoms and recurrent infections; ENT review and grommets prevent hearing-related developmental delay
Connect with peer support communities (Cleft Lip and Palate Association, AmeriFace, Smile Train family network) for emotional support and practical guidance
Brief school staff each year on the child's needs — most children with clefts integrate fully into mainstream education

recommended: Breast milk where feasible — expressed and fed via specialty cleft bottle when direct breastfeeding is not possible
Standard infant formula via specialty cleft bottle if breast milk is not available
Solid foods introduced on a normal age timeline (~6 months) with softer textures initially
Calorie-dense supplements may be needed in infants with slower weight gain — under cleft team dietitian guidance
Normal toddler and childhood diet after palate repair, with cleft-team-friendly dental hygiene
avoid: Hard sharp foods (toast crusts, raw carrots, hard candy) in the immediate postoperative period after palate or lip repair
Use of regular bottle nipples or pacifiers in the first weeks after palate repair
Sugary drinks and snacks that promote dental caries at the cleft site
Foods that require deep suction (some thicker straws, hard tubing) in young children with unrepaired palate
Maternal smoking and alcohol during pregnancy in any future pregnancies — single largest preventable factor for sibling recurrence

exerciseGuidelines: Children with cleft lip and palate participate fully in normal physical activity and sports as they grow. After each surgical procedure, restrictions are short — typically 2-3 weeks of avoiding rough play, contact sports, and items that may impact the face. Long-term, no exercise restrictions apply. School physical education and team sports are encouraged.
dailyManagement: Use the specialty cleft feeding bottle correctly at every feed and keep cleft team feeding contact information accessible
Brush teeth twice daily with a soft pediatric brush; pediatric dental review every 6 months and at every cleft team visit
Attend all scheduled cleft team appointments — typically every 6-12 months in early childhood and yearly thereafter
Practice speech home exercises as prescribed by the speech-language pathologist
Apply scar massage and silicone sheeting as taught for at least 6 months after lip repair to optimise scar appearance
Monitor hearing — report any new ear pain, drainage, or fluid behind the eardrum and attend audiology reviews
Keep a written care diary of operations, hospital admissions, hearing results, and speech assessments to support continuity of care during transitions
prognosis: With coordinated cleft team care, long-term outcomes for children with cleft lip and palate are excellent. Over 90% achieve a satisfactory cosmetic result after primary lip repair, although 10-30% have minor lip or nasal revisions during childhood or adolescence. Speech is intelligible in over 80% of children by school age, with the rest benefiting from secondary speech procedures and ongoing therapy. Dental and orthodontic outcomes are comparable to non-cleft children when bone grafting and orthodontics are completed appropriately. Educational attainment, employment rates, and adult psychosocial functioning are similar to the general population in studies of well-supported cleft cohorts. Mortality is essentially confined to syndromic forms with additional anomalies; isolated cleft lip and palate carries no direct mortality risk. The strongest prognostic predictors are early initiation of feeding support, completion of primary surgical repairs on schedule, adherence to speech therapy, and long-term cleft team follow-up through skeletal maturity. Outcomes are significantly worse in low- and middle-income settings where access to multidisciplinary care is limited; international partnerships (Smile Train, Operation Smile) have substantially closed this gap.
recoveryTimeline: After lip repair (3-6 months), most infants return home within 24-48 hours and feed normally within a few days; the scar matures over 6-12 months. After palate repair (9-18 months), hospital stay is typically 2-4 days with elbow restraints and soft-food diet for 2-3 weeks; full healing takes 4-6 weeks. After alveolar bone graft (age 7-11), recovery is 1-2 weeks with soft diet. After Le Fort I orthognathic surgery (age 16-18), recovery includes 4-6 weeks of soft diet and full healing over 3-6 months. Speech therapy and orthodontic phases are years-long rather than discrete recovery periods. Most patients are discharged from active cleft team care by age 18-21, with adult follow-up arranged for residual concerns.
complications: Velopharyngeal insufficiency causing hypernasal speech in 10-25% after primary palate repair — managed with secondary speech surgery
Recurrent middle ear effusion and conductive hearing loss in over 90% of children with cleft palate — managed with grommets and audiology follow-up
Oronasal fistula at the palate repair site in 5-15% of cases — small fistulas may be observed; larger ones need surgical closure
Dental anomalies and malocclusion requiring orthodontic and surgical management
Maxillary hypoplasia in 25-50% of children with complete clefts — managed with orthognathic surgery at skeletal maturity
Scarring at the lip repair site requiring secondary revision in 10-30% of cases
Psychosocial challenges including teasing, low self-esteem, and anxiety — well-managed with peer support and psychological care, with most adolescents reporting outcomes comparable to peers

supportResources.name: American Cleft Palate Craniofacial Association (ACPA)
supportResources.description: US standards body for cleft team accreditation, with family resources, team directory, and educational materials.

supportResources.name: Smile Train
supportResources.description: Largest international cleft charity, providing free surgery and team care in 90+ countries with focus on low- and middle-income settings.

supportResources.name: Operation Smile
supportResources.description: International medical charity providing cleft surgery and team care worldwide; useful family resource and patient stories.

supportResources.name: CLAPA — Cleft Lip and Palate Association (UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient charity with parent support, peer mentoring, and detailed clinical resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Down Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Down syndrome is a chromosomal disorder (trisomy 21) with characteristic facial features, cognitive impairment, and frequent congenital heart disease; cleft lip and palate is occasionally part of the picture but is not the defining feature. Diagnosis is established by karyotype or chromosomal microarray.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tetralogy of Fallot
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tetralogy of Fallot is a congenital cardiac malformation, not a craniofacial condition. The two can coexist when both are part of a syndrome such as 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge), which features cleft palate, cardiac anomalies, hypocalcemia, and immune deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bilateral Cleft Lip (without palate)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated bilateral cleft lip without involvement of the palate is a distinct entity requiring lip-only repair. Combined cleft lip and palate involves both structures and follows a longer multidisciplinary care pathway including palatoplasty and ear tubes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cleft Palate (without cleft lip)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated cleft palate (without cleft lip) is more common in females, more often syndromic, and more often associated with Pierre Robin sequence. The combined cleft lip and palate page covers patients with both structures affected together.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spina Bifida
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spina bifida is an open neural tube defect of the spine, unrelated to craniofacial development. Both are folate-sensitive birth defects, which is why universal folic acid supplementation reduces both.

relatedConditions.name: Unilateral Cleft Lip
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common cleft lip variant; surgical management overlaps with combined cleft lip and palate.

relatedConditions.name: Bilateral Cleft Lip
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral form with more complex repair and more frequent need for nasoalveolar molding.

relatedConditions.name: Cleft Palate (without cleft lip)
relatedConditions.relevance: Isolated palate cleft; shares palatoplasty, speech therapy, and hearing surveillance pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Down Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: May coexist with cleft as part of broader syndromic presentation; shared genetic counselling pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Tetralogy of Fallot
relatedConditions.relevance: Congenital cardiac anomaly that may coexist when cleft is part of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome.

faqs.question: What causes cleft lip and palate?
faqs.answer: Most cleft lip and palate cases result from a combination of multiple genetic variants plus environmental influences during weeks 4-12 of pregnancy. Established risk factors include maternal smoking, alcohol, certain medications (valproate, isotretinoin), pre-existing diabetes, folate deficiency, and family history. About 30% of cleft lip and 50% of isolated cleft palate cases are part of a recognised genetic syndrome.

faqs.question: When is cleft lip surgery performed?
faqs.answer: Primary cleft lip repair is typically performed at age 3-6 months when the infant has reached the rule-of-tens (10 lb weight, 10 g/dL hemoglobin, 10 weeks old). The Millard rotation-advancement technique is most common for unilateral clefts, and Mulliken or other techniques are used for bilateral clefts. Hospital stay is usually 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: When is cleft palate surgery performed?
faqs.answer: Cleft palate repair (palatoplasty) is typically performed by 9-18 months of age, before speech development. Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty and intravelar veloplasty are the dominant techniques. Hospital stay is typically 2-4 days, with soft-food diet for 2-3 weeks. Earlier repair supports better speech outcomes; very early repair may impact mid-face growth.

faqs.question: Can cleft lip and palate be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Cleft lip is detected on routine prenatal ultrasound in approximately 30-65% of cases, typically at the 18-22 week anomaly scan. 3D ultrasound improves detection. Isolated cleft palate (without cleft lip) is much harder to identify prenatally — under 10% are detected. Prenatal diagnosis allows family preparation and referral to a fetal medicine specialist before birth.

faqs.question: Will my baby be able to breastfeed?
faqs.answer: Babies with cleft lip alone can often breastfeed with positioning support. Babies with cleft palate (with or without cleft lip) cannot generate adequate suction for breastfeeding and need a specialty cleft bottle (Haberman, Pigeon, Dr Brown's). Many mothers express breast milk to feed through the specialty bottle. Cleft team feeding specialists train parents in the first days of life.

faqs.question: Is cleft lip and palate hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a heritable component, although most cases are multifactorial. If one parent has a cleft, the chance of an affected child is 2-4%. If a sibling has a non-syndromic cleft, recurrence risk is 3-5%. Recurrence risk is higher in syndromic forms — genetic counselling provides specific estimates after detailed family history and testing.

faqs.question: How is cleft palate diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Cleft palate is diagnosed by direct examination of the infant's mouth at birth — a gloved finger sweep of the hard and soft palate detects clefts and submucous clefts that may not be visible. Submucous clefts may also be suggested by a bifid uvula or a palpable notch in the hard palate. Earlier diagnosis is rare on prenatal ultrasound.

faqs.question: Will my child need many surgeries?
faqs.answer: Most children with cleft lip and palate need 4-10 procedures across childhood and adolescence: lip repair, palate repair, ear tubes, alveolar bone graft, possible speech surgery, possible jaw surgery, and lip or nose revision. Each individual procedure recovers in days to weeks, and the overall pathway is well-tolerated with multidisciplinary support.

faqs.question: Will my child have normal speech?
faqs.answer: Over 80% of children with isolated cleft palate develop intelligible speech by school age with timely palate repair and speech-language therapy. Persistent hypernasality occurs in 10-25% and is treated with secondary speech surgery (pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty). Long-term speech outcomes depend heavily on therapy adherence and team experience.

faqs.question: Will my child have hearing problems?
faqs.answer: Eustachian tube dysfunction is near-universal in cleft palate, leading to middle ear effusion and conductive hearing loss in over 90% of affected children. Ventilation tubes (grommets) drain the middle ear and ventilate the space, usually placed at the time of palate repair and replaced as needed. Hearing is closely monitored throughout childhood with regular audiology review.

faqs.question: Will my child have dental problems?
faqs.answer: Children with clefts commonly have missing, extra, or malformed teeth at the cleft site, malocclusion, and crowding. Pediatric dentists with cleft expertise and orthodontists are part of the cleft team. Most children have orthodontic treatment in mixed dentition (age 7-10) and again as teenagers, with excellent functional and cosmetic outcomes.

faqs.question: What is nasoalveolar molding (NAM)?
faqs.answer: Nasoalveolar molding is a custom-fitted oral plate worn for 3-4 months before lip repair to bring the cleft segments closer together, reshape the nasal cartilage, and elongate the columella. It is used selectively for wide unilateral and bilateral clefts. Studies show improved nasal symmetry and reduced need for secondary nasal surgery.

faqs.question: Can cleft lip and palate be prevented?
faqs.answer: Some risk factors can be reduced. Take 400-1,000 µg folic acid daily from at least 1 month before conception, stop smoking and alcohol before pregnancy, optimise diabetes control, review teratogenic medications with a clinician, and achieve healthy weight before pregnancy. Genetic factors cannot be prevented but can be addressed with preconception counselling.

faqs.question: How common is cleft lip and palate?
faqs.answer: Cleft lip and palate occurs in approximately 1 in 700-800 live births worldwide. About 7,000 US infants are born with orofacial clefts each year. Rates are highest in American Indian and Asian populations (over 1.5 per 1,000) and lower in African populations (about 0.5 per 1,000). It is the most common craniofacial birth defect globally.

faqs.question: What is a cleft team?
faqs.answer: A cleft team is a multidisciplinary group of specialists managing children with cleft lip and palate. The team includes plastic or maxillofacial surgeons, orthodontists, ENT specialists, speech-language pathologists, audiologists, pediatric dentists, geneticists, nurses, and psychologists. Team-based care delivers significantly better speech, dental, and psychosocial outcomes than fragmented care.

faqs.question: Will my child look normal after surgery?
faqs.answer: Most children achieve good or excellent cosmetic results after primary lip and palate repair. Modern surgical techniques produce a healed lip scar that becomes inconspicuous over months. About 10-30% of children have minor lip or nasal revisions in childhood or adolescence, and many have a final rhinoplasty at skeletal maturity. The majority of adults report appearance comparable to peers.

faqs.question: What is the cost of cleft surgery?
faqs.answer: In high-income countries with national or private insurance coverage, cleft team care is typically covered through childhood. In the US, total childhood costs (across multiple surgeries, orthodontics, speech therapy) can exceed USD 100,000 but are generally covered by insurance. In low-income countries, charitable programmes such as Smile Train and Operation Smile fund free surgery and team care.

faqs.question: Is cleft lip and palate associated with intellectual disability?
faqs.answer: Isolated non-syndromic cleft lip and palate is not associated with intellectual disability — affected children have normal intelligence and reach normal developmental milestones. When intellectual disability is present, it suggests a syndromic cause such as 22q11.2 deletion, Down syndrome, or fetal alcohol spectrum, which is identified through genetic and developmental assessment.

faqs.question: When can my child go to school?
faqs.answer: Children with cleft lip and palate attend school on the standard timeline. Most attend mainstream nursery, primary, and secondary education. Brief school visits by the cleft team and clear communication with teachers help. Most children participate fully in academics, sports, and social life. Speech therapy may continue during school hours through age 8-10 in some cases.

faqs.question: Are there support groups for families?
faqs.answer: Yes. Major patient charities include the American Cleft Palate Craniofacial Association (ACPA), CLAPA in the UK, Smile Train, Operation Smile, and AmeriFace. They offer peer-to-peer connection, parent mentoring, school resources, and information for affected adults. Most cleft teams also host annual family days and structured psychological support sessions.
